name: Paro
summary: Paro is exceptionally safe for tourists, with very low crime rates and a peaceful Buddhist culture. The main concerns are natural hazards like floods and earthquakes, and minor tourist scams. The presence of mandatory guides enhances visitor safety.
quickVerdict: Paro stands out as one of the safest destinations in Asia for travelers. The deeply religious Buddhist culture, low crime rates, and regulated tourism system create an exceptionally secure environment. Violent crime is virtually non-existent, and petty theft is rare. The mandatory guide system provides additional security. Natural hazards from floods and earthquakes present the main risks, while visitors should remain aware of minor scams targeting tourists in popular areas.

neighborhoods.name: Paro Town Center
neighborhoods.description: Safe and well-patrolled main town area with shops, restaurants, and tourist facilities. Good street lighting and regular police presence.

neighborhoods.name: Tiger's Nest Trail Area
neighborhoods.description: Generally safe hiking area with regular foot traffic and guide supervision. Some risk from altitude and trail conditions during monsoons.

neighborhoods.name: Airport Road
neighborhoods.description: Well-maintained road connecting airport to town center. Safe for pedestrians with adequate lighting and regular taxi services.

neighborhoods.name: Rinpung Dzong Area
neighborhoods.description: Extremely safe religious and administrative center with heavy security presence. Well-maintained and closely monitored by authorities.

neighborhoods.name: Rural Villages
neighborhoods.description: Very safe traditional farming communities with strong community bonds. Extremely low crime rates but limited infrastructure during emergencies.

neighborhoods.name: Riverside Areas
neighborhoods.description: Generally safe but prone to flooding during monsoon season. Some areas have limited lighting at night and potential for swift water hazards.

scams.name: Overpriced Handicrafts
scams.description: Tourist shops charging inflated prices for traditional handicrafts and souvenirs, especially near major attractions.
scams.howToAvoid: Compare prices at multiple shops, bargain respectfully, and ask your guide for recommended authentic stores with fair pricing.

scams.name: Fake Tour Guides
scams.description: Unauthorized individuals posing as official guides, offering services without proper credentials or Tourism Council registration.
scams.howToAvoid: Only use guides registered with the Tourism Council of Bhutan. Verify credentials and ask for official identification before hiring.

scams.name: Taxi Overcharging
scams.description: Some taxi drivers may quote higher fares to tourists or take longer routes to increase costs.
scams.howToAvoid: Agree on fares beforehand, use official taxi services, and consider having your guide arrange transportation to ensure fair pricing.

scams.name: Restaurant Bill Inflation
scams.description: Some establishments may overcharge tourists for food and drinks, especially those without clear menu pricing.
scams.howToAvoid: Always check menu prices before ordering, ask for the bill to be itemized, and dine at recommended restaurants with transparent pricing.

soloFemale.overview: Paro is exceptionally safe for solo female travelers, ranking among the world's safest destinations for women. The Buddhist culture emphasizes respect for women, and harassment is extremely rare. The mandatory guide system provides additional security, and locals are known for their hospitality and helpfulness. Women enjoy significant freedom and equality in Bhutanese society.
tips: Dress modestly, especially when visiting religious sites - cover shoulders and knees
Take advantage of the guide system for additional safety and cultural insights
Stay in well-reviewed accommodations and inform someone of your itinerary
Respect local customs like removing shoes in temples and walking clockwise around religious structures
Carry cash as credit cards are not widely accepted outside major hotels

nightSafety.overview: Paro is very safe at night with low crime rates and a peaceful atmosphere. The town has a quiet nightlife scene with several bars and restaurants that cater to tourists. Street lighting may be limited in some areas, but violent crime is virtually non-existent. Most establishments close early, creating a calm environment after dark.
tips: Stick to well-lit main roads and avoid unlit riverside paths at night
Use hotel transport or arranged taxis rather than walking alone late at night
Enjoy the local nightlife but remember most venues close early by Western standards
If going out, inform your guide or hotel staff of your plans and expected return time
Be aware that public transportation is very limited after dark

transport.metro: Paro does not have a metro system. Public transportation is limited to buses and shared vehicles connecting to other towns.
transport.rideshare: No international rideshare apps operate in Paro. Local taxi services and hotel arrangements are the primary options for private transport.
transport.taxis: Taxis are generally safe and reliable. Drivers are licensed and vehicles undergo regular safety inspections. Agree on fares beforehand or use official taxi stands at the airport and main town areas.
transport.tips: Book taxis through your hotel or guide for best safety and fair pricing. Roads can be narrow and winding, so choose experienced local drivers familiar with mountain driving conditions.

customs: Remove shoes before entering temples, monasteries, and traditional homes
Walk clockwise around stupas, chortens, and religious structures
Use both hands or right hand when giving or receiving items, especially gifts or money
Dress modestly covering shoulders and knees, particularly at religious sites
Don't point with your finger - use an open palm or gesture with your chin
Show respect for the King and royal family - avoid negative comments
Don't touch anyone's head as it's considered sacred
Ask permission before photographing people or inside religious buildings

health.overview: Healthcare in Paro includes a 40-bed general hospital and several basic health units. For complex medical issues, patients are referred to the national referral hospital in Thimphu or facilities in India. The government provides free healthcare to citizens.
health.water: Tap water is not recommended for tourists. Drink bottled water or properly boiled water. Most hotels provide safe drinking water.
health.vaccinations: Hepatitis A, Hepatitis B, and Typhoid vaccinations are recommended. Ensure routine vaccinations are up to date. Malaria risk exists in some lower elevation areas.
health.altitude: Paro sits at approximately 2,200 meters elevation. Some visitors may experience mild altitude sickness. Stay hydrated, avoid overexertion initially, and ascend gradually to higher elevations.

emergency.usEmbassy: US Embassy services are provided through New Delhi, India. Contact: +91 11 2419-8000. Bhutan Mission to UN in New York: +1 212 682-2268

faq.a: Yes, Paro is exceptionally safe for tourists with very low crime rates, a peaceful Buddhist culture, and regulated tourism that includes mandatory guides. It's considered one of the safest destinations in Asia.
Paro is very safe at night with minimal crime. The town has a quiet atmosphere after dark with limited nightlife. Stay on well-lit main roads and inform your hotel of evening plans.

faq.q: Is Paro safe for solo female travelers?
faq.a: Absolutely. Paro is one of the world's safest destinations for solo female travelers. The Buddhist culture emphasizes respect for women, harassment is extremely rare, and the mandatory guide system provides additional security.

faq.q: What areas should I avoid in Paro?
faq.a: There are no particularly dangerous areas in Paro. Exercise caution near riverside areas during monsoon season due to flooding risk, and avoid unlit paths at night. Remote hiking areas should be explored with guides.

faq.q: Is it safe to use public transport in Paro?
faq.a: Public transport is limited but generally safe. Taxis are reliable and drivers are licensed. Agree on fares beforehand and use hotel-arranged transport when possible. Roads can be challenging so choose experienced local drivers.
